---
title: "Structuring Results for Analysis"
---

# Structuring results for Analysis

So far, most of our outputs have been returned as lists. This is intentional, as **direct printing of data frames is not possible in LISSY** for security reasons.

Nevertheless, structuring results in a tabular format (rows × columns) ensures compatibility with standard data workflows, making it straightforward to integrate the results into downstream tasks such as visualization, or modelling.

In addition, a tabular structure makes the data easier to export, allowing users to continue further analysis, as well as to reuse and inspect the data more effectively.


<br>

## `structure_to_plot()`

The function `structure_to_plot()` was designed to convert complex outputs into a format suitable for analysis and visualization. Specifically, it transforms the results produced by the `lissyrtools::run_weighted_*()` family of functions, from nested lists, **into tidy tabular data frames in long format**.

To illustrate this, and to provide a visual representation of the resulting output, we present examples **using `lissyrtools` locally** with some of its embedded [sample datasets](https://lis-cross-national-data-center.github.io/lissyrtools/reference/index.html#sample-datasets). This approach reflects how a user would interact with `lissyrtools` outside of LISSY, working on their local machine, which is made possible through the inclusion of these embedded datasets.

<!-- This part is for users to run in LISSY not to be eval .....  -->

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold', message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
library(dplyr)
library(purrr)

available_sample_datasets = c(
    'it14', 'it16', 'it20',
    'mx14', 'mx16', 'mx18',
    'us14', 'us16', 'us18'
)
datasets_final <- lissyuse(
  data = available_sample_datasets, 
  vars = c('educ', 'pilabour', 'age', 'emp', 'sex'), 
  from = 2014, 
  to = 2018  
  ) 
```

<!--  .... but .....  here we run sample datasets ....  -->

<!--  .... then we stored them in an object called 'datasets_final'   -->

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE, include = FALSE}
source('../src/mimic_sample_datasets.R')
```

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold', message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
output_as_list <- datasets_final %>% 
  purrr::map(~ .x %>% 
    filter(age>23 & age <56) %>% 
    filter(emp ==1)
  ) %>%
  apply_ppp_adjustment(var_name = 'pilabour', database = 'lis', transformation = 'lisppp', base_year_ppp = 2021) %>%
  run_weighted_mean('pilabour')

print(output_as_list)  
```

Now that we have the aggregate figures in a familiar format, we simply need to pass this object to the `structure_to_plot()` function to seamlessly restructure it into a tidy data frame, as depicted below:

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold', message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
lissyrtools::structure_to_plot(output_as_list)   
```

![](/images/stp_1.png){fig-align="center"}


If the user performs a more disaggregated analysis that goes beyond a simple figure by country and year, by instead adding a subgroup layer through the `by` argument in some of the `run_weighted_*()` functions, `structure_to_plot()` also accommodates those cases automatically.

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold', message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
datasets_final %>% 
  map(~ .x %>% filter(age>23 & age <56) %>% filter(emp ==1)) %>%
  apply_ppp_adjustment(var_name = 'pilabour', database = 'lis', transformation = 'lisppp', base_year_ppp = 2021) %>% 
  run_weighted_mean('pilabour', by = 'educ') %>% 
  structure_to_plot()
```

![](/images/stp_2.png){fig-align="center"}


Finally, it also goes a step further by accommodating figures for the indicator of interest across the distribution within a given subgroup for each country and year, making the process really user-friendly

<!--  .... cumbersome I know   -->

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold', message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
datasets_final %>% 
  map(~ .x %>% filter(age>23 & age <56) %>% filter(emp ==1)) %>%
  apply_ppp_adjustment(var_name = 'pilabour', database = 'lis', transformation = 'lisppp', base_year_ppp = 2021) %>% 
  run_weighted_percentiles('pilabour', by = 'educ', average = TRUE) %>% 
  structure_to_plot()
```

![](/images/stp_3.png){fig-align="center"}

::: callout-caution
Reminder that these examples are run locally using sample datasets, which are a subset of datasets available in LIS, specifically from countries where access to the microdata is publicly accessible.
:::

With the data properly restructured into a tabular format, next we will show how to export it directly from the LISSY console into a CSV file, as well as how to plot it within LISSY.
